source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.2.2 Creating a tar backup (pp. 178-181)

questions.q: Create an uncompressed backup of /home into /backups/home.133.tar in RELATIVE mode.
questions.why: cvf: c creates, v shows processed files, f names the archive. tar backs up in relative mode by default even if you give an absolute path.

questions.q: What do the tar keys c, v and f mean?
options: c = create a backup, v = show the processed file names (verbose), f = specify the archive file name
c = compress, v = verify, f = force
c = copy, v = view, f = find
c = check, v = validate, f = format
questions.why: c creates, v is verbose, f names the archive/medium; the leading hyphen is optional in tar.

questions.q: Create an uncompressed backup of /home into /backups/home.133.P.tar explicitly in ABSOLUTE mode.
questions.why: The P key keeps the absolute paths; without both P AND an absolute source path, the backup is relative.

questions.q: What does the P key change, and what is required for it to work?
options: It stores absolute paths; you must also give the source path as absolute
It compresses the archive; nothing else is needed
It previews the archive without creating it
questions.why: P preserves absolute paths, but only if the source is also written absolutely; otherwise tar falls back to relative mode.

questions.q: Create a gzip-compressed backup of dirname/ called backup.tar.gz.
questions.why: The z key adds gzip compression on top of the create keys.

questions.q: Create a bzip2-compressed backup of dirname/ called backup.tar.bz2.
questions.why: The j key adds bzip2 compression; .tbz and .tb2 are equivalent to .tar.bz2.

questions.q: Estimate (in bytes) the size an uncompressed tar of /directory/to/backup/ would be, without writing a file.
questions.why: Writing the archive to '-' (stdout) and piping to 'wc -c' counts the bytes without creating a file (use bash, not zsh).
